one_line_takeaway: ArcMark reformulates multi-bit LLM watermarking as channel coding with side information, deriving a Shannon capacity for distortion-free watermarking and an optimal-transport-based construction that provably achieves that capacity under simplifying assumptions, empirically outperforming the BiMark baseline in message accuracy, perplexity-preservation, and robustness to back-translation paraphrasing across Llama3-8B, Qwen3-8B, and Mistral-7B -- while ArcMark and BiMark reach comparable near-perfect zero-bit detection at longer text lengths.
key_finding: Table 1 (Qwen3-8B perplexity, averaged over 1000 trials for 2-/3-byte and 500 for 4-byte payloads) shows ArcMark's perplexity tracks the no-watermark baseline much more closely than BiMark's across token lengths 50-300 and payload sizes 2-4 bytes -- e.g. at 300 tokens, no-watermark perplexity is 3.941 +/- 0.037, ArcMark is 3.878 +/- 0.039 (2-byte) / 3.821 +/- 0.039 (3-byte) / 3.887 +/- 0.056 (4-byte), while BiMark is 4.148 +/- 0.042 / 4.116 +/- 0.043 / 4.189 +/- 0.059 respectively for the same payloads -- i.e., ArcMark's perplexity is within ~1-2% of the unwatermarked baseline while BiMark's is roughly 5-7% higher, consistent with (but not formally implied by) the theoretical distortion-free guarantee. On message accuracy (Fig. 3, Qwen3-8B, 2/3/4-byte payloads), ArcMark is reported to be comparable or superior to BiMark and MPAC across all settings, with the performance gap widening as payload size grows, attributed to ArcMark's joint (whole-message) minimum-distance decoding versus BiMark's per-token majority voting. Under the BT paraphrasing attack (Fig. 4, 3-byte payload, r in {0.1,0.2,0.3} on all three models), ArcMark retains substantially higher message accuracy than BiMark at every attack ratio, and notably, attacked ArcMark at r=0.3 still outperforms unattacked BiMark on all three models. For zero-bit detection (Appendix K, TPR at 1% FPR), the paper explicitly reports that BiMark achieves modestly higher TPR at shorter text lengths, but both methods reach near-perfect detection from 150 tokens onward -- i.e., this is the one axis where the paper does not claim ArcMark strictly dominates BiMark. On downstream task quality (SQuAD v2, LCC, HumanEval, 2-byte messages), the paper states ArcMark closely matches the unwatermarked baseline across all three tasks and both models, indicating no meaningful degradation, though the specific numeric scores are deferred to Appendix J (not present in the extracted first-22-page text).
core_contribution: The paper introduces ArcMark, a distortion-free multi-bit (multi-byte) LLM watermarking scheme that recasts multi-bit watermarking as a channel-coding-with-side-information problem rather than the token-by-token bit assignment used by prior methods. Concretely: (1) it defines a formal information-theoretic model of distortion-free multi-bit watermarking and derives the Shannon capacity of this channel (Theorem 4.1), giving a closed-form capacity expression for a specific class of near-deterministic ('binary-support') next-token distributions (Corollary 4.2, capacity approaching ~0.2787 bits/token as vocabulary size N->infinity); (2) it constructs ArcMark itself: a random linear channel code maps a k-bit message to a codeword symbol per token, the codeword symbol plus shared side information (a random shift and a random permutation of the vocabulary) is mapped to a target angle on the unit circle, and an optimal-transport (Sinkhorn) step biases the LLM's next-token distribution toward tokens near that angle while provably preserving the marginal token distribution (the distortion-free constraint in Eq. 1); (3) it proves (Theorem 4.3) that under the same simplifying binary-support assumption, ArcMark with specific parameter choices (p=r=N, phi=pi/2N, a particular score function f) achieves the derived capacity; (4) it decodes messages via minimum-distance decoding over all candidate codewords using angular distance, optimizing for whole-message accuracy rather than per-bit accuracy.
threat_model: The paper's own security-relevant evaluation is narrow and explicit: robustness of the embedded watermark message to a back-translation (BT) paraphrasing attack, where each sentence of the generated text is independently passed through an English->French->English machine-translation pipeline (Helsinki-NLP OPUS-MT) at attack ratios r in {0.1, 0.2, 0.3} applied per-sentence. This models an adversary/downstream user who paraphrases watermarked text (partially) to try to degrade or remove the embedded multi-bit signal while presumably preserving semantic content. The paper does not model a fully adaptive or white-box adversary, does not evaluate token-substitution, deletion, or insertion attacks explicitly in the main text (the Limitations section flags 'explicit robustness to adversarial edits such as text substitution and deletion' as future work, though the earlier abstract text mentions attacks that alter a subset of the LLM text -- this is discussed but the concrete attack tested is only back-translation paraphrasing). There is no cryptographic security proof of unforgeability/undetectability against a keyless adversary; the paper is fundamentally a communication/coding-theoretic construction with an empirical robustness evaluation, not an adversarial security analysis.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Conclusion/Limitations section): (1) the capacity analysis (Theorem 4.1, Corollary 4.2, Theorem 4.3) relies on an i.i.d.-across-time assumption on next-token distributions, which the authors state is necessary for theoretical tractability ('single-letterization') but is not required for ArcMark's practical operation; (2) the closed-form capacity result (Corollary 4.2) and the capacity-achieving parameter choices (Theorem 4.3) are derived only for the restrictive class of distributions uniform on pairs of tokens (P2(X)) -- extending the capacity characterization to broader, more realistic next-token distribution classes is left as future work; (3) the framework does not yet provide explicit robustness guarantees against adversarial edits such as text substitution and deletion (only back-translation paraphrasing is empirically tested); (4) the practical scheme in the main experiments does not use the theoretically capacity-achieving parameters (phi=0 and identity f are used instead, for simplicity), so the reported empirical gains are not a direct empirical validation of the capacity-achieving theorem; (5) three cited distortion-free multi-bit competitors (MC2Mark, MirrorMark, StealthInk) could not be benchmarked because they lack public implementations, so the empirical comparison set is limited to BiMark (primary) and MPAC (non-distortion-free reference only); (6) the zero-bit detection axis shows BiMark modestly outperforming ArcMark at short text lengths, an exception to the paper's general 'ArcMark dominates' framing that the authors report without downplaying.

datasets_used: LCC / LongCoder dataset (long-context Python code completion, downstream utility)
Human-written C4 articles (negatives for zero-bit detection)
quantitative_results: Corollary 4.2 capacity for binary-support (P2(X)) token distributions approaches ~0.2787 bits/token as vocabulary size N -> infinity
Qwen3-8B perplexity at 50 tokens: no-watermark 4.661 +/- 0.058; ArcMark 4.758/4.738/4.696 +/- (2/3/4-byte); BiMark 4.967/4.976/4.965 +/- (2/3/4-byte)
Message accuracy trials: 1000 trials each for 1-, 2-, 3-byte payloads; 500 trials for 4-byte payloads, with SEM error bars, across Llama3-8B, Mistral-7B, Qwen3-8B

BT paraphrasing attack evaluated at ratios r in {0.1, 0.2, 0.3} for a 3-byte payload, 1000 trials per condition, on Qwen3-8B, Llama3-8B, Mistral-7B; ArcMark at r=0.3 (attacked) still outperforms BiMark at r=0 (unattacked) on all three models
Zero-bit detection reported as TPR at fixed FPR=1%; BiMark modestly higher TPR at shorter text lengths, both methods near-perfect from 150 tokens onward
Side-information resolution ablation over r in {64, 256, 512} shows similar message accuracy, motivating the default r=64 used in main experiments
Side information construction: 31-bit secret key via PCG64 PRNG, SHA-256 hash of key concatenated with Unicode-normalized text of 3 preceding tokens
baselines_compared: BiMark (Feng et al. 2025) -- primary baseline, distortion-free, k sequential green/red reweighting layers with majority-vote decoding
MPAC (Yoo, Ahn, Kwak 2024) -- non-distortion-free, included only as a reference point, not held to the distortion-free standard
Unwatermarked baseline (no watermark) -- for perplexity and downstream task comparisons
Three distortion-free multi-bit methods (MC2Mark, MirrorMark, StealthInk-style construction) cited but explicitly NOT benchmarked due to lack of public implementations
